purpose: Identifies how structural labor scarcity fundamentally changes default dynamics during the 2026-2029 leveraged loan maturity wall
components: ~60% in sectors where labor is 45-70% of operating costs
Historical default models trained when labor markets loosened during stress (providing margin relief)
Demographics removes the counter-cyclical labor relief valve
Structured hypothesis: 2-3pp default rate uplift (from 3-5% to 5-8%)
EBITDA addbacks (25-30%) embed unrealizable labor synergies
current_assessment: Analytically compelling (6/10) but empirically untested (3/10). The combination of covenant-lite dominance, structural labor scarcity, and $600B annual maturity wall has literally never occurred. No backtestable precedent exists.
limitations: No historical precedent for this specific combination of factors
The 2-3pp default rate uplift is a structured hypothesis, not an empirical finding
AI and automation could partially substitute for scarce labor
Interest rate environment at refinancing is a major swing factor not captured by demographic analysis alone
Sector-specific immigration carve-outs could partially relieve labor scarcity
